parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: This recording is copyright. You will hear a guide welcoming visitors to a museum, which shows how ordinary people lived and worked in the past in a town called Arvington.

Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen. Let me tell you about some of the areas you'll walk through.

Speaker 1: The museum is set up so that you walk through displays that take you back through different periods in Arvington's history and see how people lived. Starting with the forge, you can go back 1,000 years in time and see how skilled workers crafted everyday objects out of wood and metal.

Speaker 1: But we don't just tell you about it. We get you to do it yourself, so you'll get experience of carefully crafting a useful object, like a knife.

Speaker 1: And then in the market, one of our historical interpreters will be there in person telling you what it was like to work on a farm growing produce like wheat and vegetables and then carry it all over very long distances to a market to try and sell.

Speaker 1: You can ask the interpreters questions, of course, and you'll find they're very knowledgeable.

Speaker 1: Staying with the subject of food, the next area in the museum is the bakery, where we make the kinds of foods that local people would have eaten in the past, using the original cooking techniques.

Speaker 1: These are on sale there for you to try. The recipes and cooking methods may be ancient, but 21st century forms of payment are accepted.

Speaker 1: Next on the tour is the building zone, where you have the chance to sit down to enjoy the spectacular time-lapse animated movie of Arvington developing over the last 1,000 years from a tiny village into the town we have today.

Speaker 1: It's just three minutes long, but you can view it as many times as you like.

Speaker 1: After that, you'll come to the hall, a space that's devoted to the clothing industry, which was a huge part of the economy of Arvington and the surrounding area in the past.

Speaker 1: You can find out how clothing was made when everything was done by hand. And you can have a go at helping one of our historical interpreters to make a piece of clothing yourself.

Speaker 1: So if you never knew how to sew a button back onto a shirt, you might find it useful.

Speaker 1: Finally, the tour ends down in the basement, where there's a wide variety of merchandise available, including books and toys.

Speaker 1: A final tip, download the museum app and you get an introductory discount voucher, which you can use on purchases in the basement as well as commentaries on all the exhibits in the museum.

Speaker 1: Anyway, enjoy your visit.
